author: @hellohelloington9442
text:
Amazing what you're doing here, you should definitely do this more often. Hell, you could just get a team for this and pretty much shut down all these item sales.

Though, now for a bit of a controversial take on this:
I think ROBLOX could just fix this whole problem by introducing an age restriction system for items, clothing, audio and games, rather than ceaselessly trying to moderate those who try to bypass the aforementioned items. Those people will just makes more alts and make more items, and ROBLOX can never stop them from spreading and multiplying, especially as more people from younger audiences join those communities and grow up to take part in it themselves.

If ROBLOX were to just introduce an age restriction filter, it would fix the problem for everybody. Children don't see condos and clothing like that, while those who partake in things like that are allowed to do as they will without harming the rest of the community. It's a small niche anyway, so age restricted games would probably just mostly be things like FPS games with a bit too much gore. We've already got the groundwork for that on ROBLOX, but it would be nice to see it go deeper. Sort of a live and let live system - if ROBLOX doesn't stop them and instead limits where they can be, then they will stop creating these games and items on the main platform where children can see them, and instead in their own little area. For the most part, even the condo community tries to control what age ranges play their games, so it would be a welcome change to them too.

Hopefully ROBLOX will make a change for the better and solve this problem, one way or another. There's probably better ways than what I said here, but I think that is, by far, the simplest method.


Also, about the whole nazi shirt:
ROBLOX's item approval system is entirely automated. You can bypass nearly anything by just putting 'black lives matter' on it. I'm in the clothing creation community, and whenever ROBLOX falsely bans a clothing item, we use bypass templates that just adds that sort of thing to the background of the image, and ROBLOX approves it. And, no, I don't mean this sort of clothing - ROBLOX does also ban totally legitimate clothing that doesn't break any rules, largely by mistake, due to the fact that the whole system is automated (and the only time an actual moderator sees it is when the item is reported, sold lots of times, or otherwise).

author: @Preinstallable
text:
I, as the leader of a mass condo reporting discord server, tip my hat off to you for assisting in banning some of the unbannable. That's right, some of these damn condo games fuck with Roblox mods enough that i've seen one of them be up for >1 year without being moderated. Because the game appears "normal" on the surface. Until you join some group, then it allows access through a teleporter in game that then sends you to the condo place. So they lock down the group, basically makes it invulnerable. Until my group mass reported it. Then just recently we saw the last traces of the group be moderated fully. It took over a year and a half for roblox to delete that shit. And when they uploaded another, we kept catching up, until they found that using older hacked accounts gives the owner "protection" from moderation. So a member of our group called you. God bless, you sacrificed your access to Roblox to make it a safer place for everyone.

FUN FACT: The condo group aforementinoed uploaded furry condos, and that makes me want to die inside.

author: @savagetheunicorn4555
text:
This problem is probably going to be drowned out but hopefully you see it Ruben. There is a game owned by Ubisoft (Growtopia) and it's also a game marketed for teenagers. Since ubisoft purchased the game from the orignal devs, it's become a cesspool of illegal activity. For example, there is a world named LOVE and I see atleast 1-5 people asking for nudes/sexual conversations from/with strangers without asking age DAILY. The game is supposed to be 13+ (rated T for teen for violence, blood when there is no blood, Use of Alcohol and Tobacco, and Simulated Gambling. Despite filing countless reports against these players and directly messaging moderators about it, the issue persists and NONE of them receive bans. Out of all of the illegal actions this is the worst in my opinion and no recourse has been taken for it. I've been reporting players for around 2-4 months and no action has been taken. I fear Ubisoft will ignore this problem until it is shoved in their face via publication. Please take a look if you have the time, I don't know who else to ask. The game is silently becoming known as a safe haven for predators at this point and it terrifies me. I've been playing the game since 2013 so I can accurately recount the rise and fall of the game, including the increase in illegal and predatory actions. Gambling is a huge problem in the game and is actively fought against daily, yet nothing comes to the predators. I even tried to apply for a position that's just below moderator to have my voice heard about 3 or 4 times but I've been rejected every time so far. I have no idea what else to do, I would make a video about it myself but even if I did it would be lost in the pit called YouTube. Thanks for your time, love the videos. Keep up the great work.

I'd love to join a team to help you, any way to start this process?

author: @Bigol_Jimbo
text:
Honestly... I'm just shocked how Roblox hasn't been sued into nonexistence at this point...

You'd think in the very least that some country's consumer protection agency would catch wind of "big company making tons of money off of children doesn't give enough of a shit to keep said children safe from being groomed on their platform" and immediately pull an Australia and ban half of the steam store along with it.

I can understand there's some nuance to the issue, where if they get too restrictive like back in the day then it prevents people from making some great stuff... but you'd think if they're making enough money to just blow it on attacking some random content creator that they could divert that money to some more human oversight.

text: I'm convinced at this point that human moderators don't actually do >90% of the moderation on Roblox anymore, they just have an AI set up to check the items for 'inappropriate' content, and if anything's flagged a mod will check it and choose to delete it or not. Problem is, the AI itself is terrible and usually can't find anything wrong with these items because of basic avoidance techniques (it's why some items were mixed in with skin colors, by using that body color the item would look normal, thus being bypassed), and this AI _especially_ can't do anything against smarter methods, such as fragmenting something over multiple decals to avoid detection, and then layering all of them on the same surface to assemble a complete image.

author: @ScoobyMcBoobies
text: Thank God someone with an organic eye is finally looking at this stuff! It was annoying here and there, but now the whole site is getting soggy from this mess. Thank ya, Boss. Can't lie either, had the worrying realization that these might be the dudes from Second Life, IMVU, etc. Do admit it's been a hot minute since I even logged into my 'newest' IMVU so I'm unsure of usergenerated content, but I know sure as shit that some of the larger creators in Second Life were making bank. Buuuut, those games are starting to fade into obscura. Probably cause they never bothered with adding more content besides social stuff - clothing, accessories, name emblems and the like. But Roblox has always been about games! Which means that the base itself had more than enough content, and all the jazz that came after was just bonus stuff. So the revenue will, technically, only stop when people stop making games on there. And just like others games prior, they've began to allow UGC. Main guys from the others games know that, and they want some of the cake, but the issue is, is that they're producing the same stuff. It's all for profit without any emotional attachment towards the consumer. And people can try and say it's for the funny, but that ain't funny. Memes are funny, jokes are funny. Stencil tits on a Lego person? Not even worth the chuckle. Then some get mad at you for the reaction, and it's like, "Damn, dude, who shit on your sundae?"

Furries have good and bad, like with most things

Never huddle a book by its cover

Or

You don’t need to follow it
Just take it to thought with you

author: @wesleyward5901
text:
God I love this man's content *so* much, Roblox should be thanking him for all the work he does.

All the furry hate is also a bonus and a massive W. Mr Gigachad over here even got banned on Twitter because the fuwwies got angwy.

I know people are saying the hate is underserved, but it's really not. Sure, not all furries are child diddlers, but if you were to tell someone who's never experienced the outside world that the LGBTQ+ community makes up 1% of the population but commit over 50% of worldwide child s*xual assault cases, they'd probably hate them. This example is probably a little skewed because numbers change over time, but it puts things into perspective.

And no, I'm not comparing furries to the LGBTQ+ society, it's just an example. All groups, no matter how big or small, have those disgusting horrible people who deserved to be locked up and depending on whether you see the good or bad side of the community depends whether you support or hate them. Also furries are weird.

author: @explorerbe632
text:
Batman is a wealthy orphan from DC’s version of Newark, New Jersey. Instead of actually trying to better the city, he uses life-long martial arts skills and anti-personnel weaponry to beat up poor people. Some of Batman’s greater known villains are mutilated former friends, former prostitutes turned thieves and morbidly obese gangsters. Don’t worry, kids. Batman beats them up and makes them pay for being “new money”. No one in Gotham is sure what that means, but they don’t stop Batman out of fear of an errant batarang.

For those wanting a serious history, the best effort will be made to piece together the major DC timelines to pound out something rather coherent. There has always been a Bat-person to protect Gotham. Whether it was a Bat caveman fighting Vandal Savage to 19th-century vigilantes protecting early Gotham City to the Batman that we know today. But, what makes Batman different than any of these vigilantes? Well, Batman has a long term plan. Bruce Wayne runs the Wayne Foundation and spends his fortune creating technology and job opportunities to eliminate the criminal base of Gotham City.

However, the powerful figureheads get to meet Batman at night. Working as a dual faced savior for the city, Batman seems like the perfect crime fighting vigilante. Well, except for the Joker killing Jason Todd, crippling Barbara Gordon, killing Sarah Essen, cutting off Alfred’s hand and gassing dozens of innocents. By the events of Batman v Superman, the audience sees a Batman that bears a close resemblance to Frank Miller’s take on the character. An aging Bruce Wayne that conveniently looks under 50 is taking to mass torture and coordinated military-style assaults against overwhelming odds. The Batmobile is decked out to resemble a military assault vehicle and the Batman himself belongs to myth.

There is nothing wrong with this take on the Batman, as it evokes the earliest of Golden Age pulpiness that powered the character for its first year. However, it’s the ease of the character’s slide into fascism that bothers some. Batman/Bruce Wayne/Batfleck is the wealthiest man in Gotham City, but he gets to skirt police authority to enforce his brand of justice on the criminal element. A criminal element that skews poor and unable to rise out of the mire unless they agree to the Wayne Foundation’s conditions. Tyranny comes on leathery wings.
